item.title: Laws of Software Engineering
item.description: A collection of principles and patterns that shape software systems, teams, and decisions.

item.title: Anthropic - OpenClaw
item.description: Anthropic builds the Claude model family and provides access via an API and Claude CLI. In OpenClaw, Anthropic API keys and Claude CLI reuse are both supported. Existing legacy Anthropic token profiles are still honored at runtime if they are already configured.

item.title: Speed Up Your AI Development Workflow by 2x
item.description:
This video is NOT sponsored. I just like the products. Online Workshop: Vibe Coding for Production: https://dometrain.com/workshop/vibe-coding-for-production/?ref=nick-chapsas&promo=mail-list&coupon_code=3YEARS
Get every Dometrain 40% off with code 3YEARS: https://dometrain.com/courses/?ref=nick-chapsas&promo=youtube&coupon_code=3YEARS
Hello, everybody. I'm Nick, and in this video, I want to talk about two applications that I am using to speed up my development workflow by 2x. They are both free to use, run locally, and are a crucial part of any developer’s toolkit.
Coyypist: https://cotypist.app (Free for now, with commercial plans in the future) TypeWhisper: https://www.typewhisper.com (Free for personal use and open source)
Don't forget to comment, like and subscribe :)
Social Media: Follow me on GitHub: https://github.com/Elfocrash Follow me on Twitter: https://twitter.com/nickchapsas Connect on LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/in/nick-chapsas
#ai #llm
item.author: (Nick Chapsas)

item.title: Kimi Vendor Verifier
item.description: Rebuilding the "Chain of Trust" -- Kimi Vendor Verifier.

item.title: The F-35 Is a Masterpiece Built for the Wrong War
item.description: Think of a violin made by a master craftsman: beautiful, precise, capable of extraordinary performance, but impossible to produce quickly or cheaply. It

item.title: Types of Board Game
How The Heck Does Shazam Work? (An Interactive Exploration)
item.description: Explore how Shazam and song identification work through interactive visualizations — spectrograms, constellation maps, hash fingerprints, and time-offset matching.

item.title: Why We Accepted Surveillance as Default — Vivian Voss
item.description: Thirty years ago two men in New York invented the third-party cookie. Google paid $3.1 billion for the company. 600 billion bid requests per second later, the question is still unanswered: what if the browser had defended the user?

item.title: Why you should work for a top tier tech company
item.description: I joined a FAANG company 1.5 years ago after working mostly in startups and one medium sized company. I wish I’d joined way sooner because I’d have millions more in my bank account. But besides the…

item.title: RAW.works - RLMs are the new reasoning models
item.author: (Raymond A. Weitekamp)

item.title: Code is the new assembly
item.description: Codex recently coded a browser from scratch. It took a week, but the AI worked solo, with no human in the loop. Could a human parse that code and truly understand it? The bigger question is: does it matter? Have you ever audited your binary’s assembly code? You don’t. You trust the compiler. AI-generated code will head in the same direction, though the journey will take time.

item.title: Three Time-to-Power Strategies that Failed in 2025
item.description: We’ve learned a lot in the last year about what won’t work for accelerating time-to-power for large data center projects.
item.author: (https://chrisgillett.org/)

item.title: The Missing Bundler Features
item.description: Over the last few months, there has been a lot of talk about making Bundler faster, both by improving it directly, or by reimplementing it in another language, and while it may surprise some, that didn’t excite me much.

item.title: Opus 4.7 — the best model nobody likes | I didn't know.
item.description: The sycophancy problem, the compute crunch, and why coding agents will not stay cheap.

item.title: ⭐Why Your First Token Is Always Late
item.description: (Part 2) The inference side of transformers, along with systems tricks that make production LLMs fast
item.author: (Anirudh Sathiya Narayanan)

item.title: Lessons from going solo
item.description: Strategies that worked for me when I left a salaried job to consult on my own.

item.title: Tim Cook to become Apple Executive Chairman John Ternus to become Apple CEO
item.description: Apple announced Tim Cook will become executive chairman of Apple’s board of directors and John Ternus will become Apple’s next CEO.

item.title: What is Canton Network (and why should you care)?
item.description: Canton Network is a layer-1 blockchain designed for regulated financial markets, addressing privacy, regulatory compliance, and interoperability without centralized control. Unlike typical public b…

item.title: When “Right vs Wrong” Isn’t Really About Truth — A Thought on Bias, Power, and Decision Making
item.description: By: Neeraj R One day, while attending a class on human bias, a simple thought experiment turned into something much deeper. It started with a very ordinary scenario. Imagine a group of 10 friends p…

item.title: Decision Graphs
item.description: About eight years ago, I wrote a little blog post on dynamic decision trees. If you have the time, go back and read that and the follow up posts on the subject. If not, I’ll summarize. Instea…

item.title: Connecting in Narratives
item.description: Missing a flight to my cruise can teach us how the right narratives invite others on the journey with us.

item.title: Uncorrelated Contents
item.description: When you take a close-up photo of a small object, there tends to be a small portion of the subject in focus, with the rest blurred out. The range of distance that's acceptably in focus is the depth of field; with macro photography it tends to be quite narrow.

item.title: I'm never buying another Kindle, and neither should you
item.description: Bricked hardware, ads, and control. The Amazon Kindle is no longer built for readers. Here’s why I’m done with it.

item.title: Why do people keep stealing the small sauce bottle?
item.description: A UX question from a restauranteur

item.title: I’m Back. Here’s my life story in a nutshell
item.description: I haven’t posted anything here in almost 3 years. The main reason is that because after a grueling job search in 2024, I lucked into joining a Big Tech / FAANG company as a senior software engineer…

item.title: Looking back on Stripe’s payment API migration
item.description: This 2020 post walks through the first and second iteration of Stripe’s payment API. The original API was designed for credit card charges and was delightfully simple. You could move money with a s…

item.title: The Ultimate Question: What Does the Endgame Look Like? - Pawel Brodzinski on Leadership in Technology
item.description: Asking "what does the endgame look like?" will show how many predictions can't hold or what other second-order consequences we will face.

item.title: Benchmarking open-weight models for security research
item.description: Testing open-weight models against unpublished vulnerabilities in real products.
item.author: (Noah Lebovic)

item.title: Language Modeling Without Neural Networks
item.description: Generating Shakespeare has become the “Hello World” of language models.1 Recently, I’ve been messing with alternative language models and came across unbounded …

item.title: Scaling Claude beyond individual workflows
item.description: A practical look at deploying Claude across an organization: skills, plugins, governance, and real-world challenges from a 23-person team.

item.title: Hauleth's blog - Scotty, I need warp speed in three minutes
item.description:
My journey into optimising Elixir codebase of Ultravisor (my fork of Supabase's Supavisor).
This story is not about a goal, but ~~friends~~ optimisations we met along the way.

item.title: Data as a Product is a Promise | Yusuf Aytas
item.description: Stop shipping tables. Build data products with clear contracts, real owners, lifecycle discipline, and observability so teams can trust data.
